one_line_takeaway: Daze shows that an adversary who controls only a simulator's state/transition dynamics -- with no ability to read or write the RL agent's rewards -- can still reliably backdoor PPO/TD3/DQN agents (matching or beating reward-poisoning attacks like TrojDRL and SleeperNets) and, notably, transfer that backdoor to real robotic hardware, while no defense currently exists against this attack surface.
key_finding: In continuous-action MuJoCo tasks at poisoning rate beta=1% and daze factor k=8, Daze achieved ASR of 92.4% (Half Cheetah), 94.1% (Hopper), and 98.5% (Reacher) with essentially no drop in Benign Return relative to an unpoisoned baseline (e.g., Hopper BR 2321.5 for Daze vs. 2085.4 for No Poisoning), while TrojDRL-C and SleeperNets-C both performed far worse on ASR in these environments (TrojDRL-C: 20.3%/25.6%/65.5%; SleeperNets-C: 3.3%/7.5%/0.0% on the same three tasks respectively). Only 0.3% of Hopper time steps were 'dazed' under Daze despite the strong ASR, indicating a small footprint. On the two custom real-robot tasks (Intersection, Waiter), Daze again matched or beat the baselines on the joint ASR/BR trade-off; notably SleeperNets-C achieved a higher raw ASR on Intersection (99.8% vs Daze's 92.3%) but at the cost of BR dropping from a clean 238.4 to 158.6, which the authors flag as easily detectable, whereas Daze's BR (226.7) stayed close to the clean baseline. The authors report what they describe as the first demonstration of an RL backdoor attack transferring successfully from simulation to real physical robot hardware (Turtlebot2 and Fetch), shown via video and via a measured shift in the real robot's linear/angular velocity distributions between triggered and untriggered runs (Figure 4). In discrete-action Atari tasks at a 0.3% poisoning rate, Daze achieved ASR of 99.3% (Q*bert), 99.9% (Frogger), 99.4% (Pacman), and 97.6% (Breakout) -- comparable to Q-Incept and SleeperNets (both near or at 100% in these tasks) and higher than TrojDRL (88.2-98.6%) -- while achieving the highest mean Benign Return of all compared methods in 3 of 4 games, despite operating with strictly less adversary access (no reward read/write) than every baseline it was compared against. Appendix results (partially visible in the extracted text) further show Daze transfers to TD3 (near-100% ASR on all three MuJoCo tasks with no BR drop) and to DQN on Atari (ASR above 90% at beta=1%), and an ablation over beta and k on Hopper shows ASR generally increasing with daze factor k (e.g., k=16 reaching 95.16% ASR at beta=0.25%, versus only 29.69% at k=1).
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new threat model in the DRL (deep reinforcement learning) supply chain: a malicious simulator developer who backdoors agents purely by manipulating the simulator's state/transition function, without ever reading or writing the agent's rewards (rewards are assumed to be computed externally by the victim, as is common with MuJoCo/PyBullet). Building on this threat model, the authors design 'Daze,' a reward-free, universal backdoor attack that works by (1) defining a trigger function that maps a subset of states into disjoint 'triggered' states with probability beta, and (2) punishing the agent for not taking the adversary's target action a+ in a triggered state by forcing it into a 'dazed' state where it is made to transition according to a uniformly random action for a number of steps proportional to its deviation from a+ (this is done purely through altered transitions, never altered rewards). If the agent instead takes a+, the wrapper performs a 'null transition' that returns the pre-trigger state, making compliance with the backdoor inconsequential to the agent's normal reward. The authors prove (Theorem 1, Theorem 2) that under a stated Assumption 1 (uniformly random actions are never part of an optimal policy in the target MDP), any policy that is optimal in the adversarial MDP M' must also achieve zero adversarial error in triggered states (attack success) and be optimal in the original benign MDP M (attack stealth). They give a concrete practical algorithm (Algorithm 1: the Daze Simulator Wrapper) implementable as an API wrapper around any base simulator, and evaluate it empirically against PPO (and, in the appendix, TD3 and DQN) agents across continuous-action MuJoCo tasks (Half Cheetah, Hopper, Reacher), two custom real/simulated robotics tasks (Turtlebot-based 'Intersection', Fetch-based 'Waiter'), and discrete-action Atari tasks (Q*bert, Frogger, Pacman, Breakout), comparing against TrojDRL, SleeperNets, and Q-Incept (the latter only in discrete domains). They additionally claim what they describe as the first demonstration of an RL backdoor attack transferring from simulation to real robotic hardware.
threat_model: Adversary role: a malicious developer/provider of an RL simulator (open- or closed-source) who wants to implant a backdoor into any agent later trained by a victim using that simulator, without knowing the victim's specific task in advance. Adversary capabilities: full control over the simulator's state observations and transition dynamics T (an 'inner loop' attacker who must decide perturbations online, before seeing future transitions), but explicitly NO read access (cannot observe agent rewards) and NO write access (cannot alter the reward function) -- rewards are computed externally by the victim (typical for MuJoCo/PyBullet-style simulators that only take actions and return next states). This is a strictly weaker/more realistic capability set than prior RL backdoor work such as TrojDRL and SleeperNets, which require either full training-pipeline control or RAM-level access to alter the replay buffer/rewards ('outer loop' attacks). Adversarial goals: (1) attack success -- the poisoned agent reliably takes a fixed target action a+ upon observing a predefined trigger state, and (2) attack stealth -- the poisoned agent's behavior/return in benign (non-triggered) states is statistically indistinguishable from a cleanly trained agent, defined formally via a value-function gap between poisoned and clean policies.
limitations: The paper's own text states: (1) theoretical guarantees rely on Assumption 1 (random actions are never optimal), which the authors acknowledge can fail for a 'small class of pathological MDPs' (e.g., ties between equally good paths), though they argue the results likely still hold in practice as long as some non-trivial fraction of states admit a suboptimal-random-action property; this is argued rather than proven for the general case. (2) The attack requires an inner-loop, online-decision adversary that cannot observe future transitions, limiting its planning relative to outer-loop attacks. (3) Q-Incept could not be included as a continuous-action baseline because its argmax-over-Q-function inception technique does not trivially extend to continuous action spaces, so the continuous-domain comparison set is TrojDRL-C/SleeperNets-C only (author-created extensions, not the original published algorithms). (4) The paper states plainly that there are currently no defenses directly applicable to preventing or mitigating Daze; existing 'universal' test-time defenses (BIRD, state sanitization) are noted to assume access to clean/uncorrupted training data as a baseline, an assumption that does not hold when the simulator itself is the adversarial component, and the authors note such defenses have separately been shown breakable by evasive triggers in prior work. (5) The authors acknowledge that Daze's perturbations can closely resemble legitimate domain-randomization and mass-parallelization techniques, which they say makes detection during training 'incredibly difficult in practice' -- an admission that their own proposed mitigation (open-source/proprietary code auditing) is only a partial, non-technical stopgap rather than a robust defense. (6) DQN results are explicitly labeled 'preliminary' by the authors.

datasets_used: MuJoCo Gymnasium tasks: Half Cheetah, Hopper, Reacher
Custom Pybullet-simulated / real-hardware tasks: Turtlebot2-based 'Intersection', Fetch-based 'Waiter'
Atari Gymnasium tasks: Q*bert, Frogger, Pacman, Breakout
quantitative_results: MuJoCo (beta=1%, k=8), Daze ASR: Half Cheetah 92.4%, Hopper 94.1%, Reacher 98.5%
MuJoCo Daze Benign Return: Half Cheetah 1627.8 vs No-Poisoning 1736.3; Hopper 2321.5 vs No-Poisoning 2085.4; Reacher -5.1 vs No-Poisoning -5.4
MuJoCo TrojDRL-C ASR: Half Cheetah 20.3%, Hopper 25.6%, Reacher 65.5%
MuJoCo SleeperNets-C ASR: Half Cheetah 3.3%, Hopper 7.5%, Reacher 0.0%
Real-robot sim-training: Daze ASR Intersection 92.3% (BR 226.7), Waiter 93.2% (BR 392.7); SleeperNets-C ASR Intersection 99.8% but BR drops to 158.6 (vs clean BR 238.4)
Discrete Atari (beta=0.3%): Daze ASR Q*bert 99.3%, Frogger 99.9%, Pacman 99.4%, Breakout 97.6%; Q-Incept and SleeperNets near/at 100% on all four; TrojDRL 88.2-98.6%
Discrete Atari Benign Return: Daze highest mean BR among poisoning methods in 3 of 4 games (e.g., Q*bert 18,052 vs Q-Incept 17,749, TrojDRL 17,113, SleeperNets 17,194, No-Poisoning 17,322)
TD3 (beta=1%, k=8): Daze ASR near 100% on all three MuJoCo tasks with no BR loss (e.g., Hopper BR 3292.3 vs No-Poisoning 2085.4)
DQN Atari: Daze ASR 96.5% (beta=0.3%) / 95.0% (beta=1%) on Q*Bert; 74.1% (beta=0.3%) / 90.1% (beta=1%) on Breakout
Hopper ablation over beta/k: ASR ranges from 25.16% (k=1, beta=2.0%) up to 95.16% (k=16, beta=0.25%)
baselines_compared: TrojDRL (Kiourti et al., 2019) and its continuous-action extension TrojDRL-C (author-created)
SleeperNets (Rathbun et al., 2024) and its continuous-action extension SleeperNets-C (author-created)
Q-Incept (Rathbun et al., 2025) -- discrete action spaces only
